title: Warehouse Integration
tagline: Live read-only warehouse previews and dev-vs-prod diffs
summary: dbt Wizard runs read-only SQL directly against the connected warehouse (Snowflake, BigQuery, and others) so an SE can preview model output and sanity-check row counts or suspicious values without a full dbt run. dbt_show resolves Jinja end to end, including ref(), source(), and macros, and executes the compiled SQL on the warehouse to return the rows a model would actually produce, which catches valid-but-wrong-data bugs that compiled SQL alone would not reveal. diff compares local dev state against production state so a change can be validated for row-count and value drift before the PR merges; per dbt Wizard's own description, it requires either dbt Cloud or deferred state (--defer --state) pointed at a production manifest.
whyDifferent: A general AI coding agent can only read the .sql file and the YAML config; it never sees a single row of data, cannot resolve a ref() chain into runnable SQL, and has no idea whether a model currently passes or what it returns. dbt Wizard executes against the live warehouse, so it answers from real rows: actual counts, actual distinct brand_name values, actual dev-vs-prod deltas. It distinguishes "the SQL parses" from "the SQL returns the right data," which file-reading and grep fundamentally cannot do.

capabilities.title: Preview without dbt run
capabilities.detail: mcp__dbt_index__warehouse issues read-only SELECTs against the live warehouse for instant row previews, row counts, and value spot-checks. No dbt build or materialization is required, so you inspect data in seconds instead of waiting on a run.

capabilities.title: Full Jinja resolution on the warehouse
capabilities.detail: mcp__dbt_index__dbt_show compiles {{ ref() }}, {{ source() }}, and custom macros into executable SQL and runs it on the warehouse, returning the exact rows a model would emit. Because it executes rather than just inspecting, it surfaces valid-but-wrong-data bugs (for example a join that should coalesce to 0 but returns null) that reading the compiled SQL would not reveal.

capabilities.title: Dev vs production drift detection
capabilities.detail: mcp__dbt_index__diff compares dev state against the production relation, surfacing row-count deltas and changed values. Per dbt Wizard's own reference, diff requires either dbt Cloud or deferred state (--defer --state) pointed at a production manifest to resolve the prod side. Either path turns a blind PR merge into an evidence-backed decision.

capabilities.title: Read-only by design
capabilities.detail: The warehouse tool issues SELECT-only statements and rejects DML/DDL, so SEs can demo live data exploration against a customer-like warehouse without write risk to the target schemas. Enforcement is at the tool layer: non-SELECT statements are rejected before they reach the warehouse, and the connection should also use a least-privilege read role as defense in depth.

capabilities.title: Deferred-state cost efficiency
capabilities.detail: diff and validation builds support --defer --state pointed at a production manifest, reusing production upstreams instead of rebuilding the whole DAG, so dev validation stays cheap even on large projects. Deferred state is one of the two ways diff resolves the prod side; dbt Cloud state is the other.

capabilities.title: Automatic post-edit validation
capabilities.detail: after any model edit the validation subagent automatically runs SQL checks, dbt run --defer, and a prod-vs-dev comparison via diff, so warehouse-backed verification happens without you asking.

examplePrompts.prompt: Show me the first 50 rows of int_orders_enriched as it would actually run, with the refs resolved.
examplePrompts.intent: Preview real model output with full Jinja resolution before building.

examplePrompts.prompt: How many rows does fct_orders have in prod right now, and how many distinct order_id?
examplePrompts.intent: Live row-count and uniqueness sanity check against the warehouse.

examplePrompts.prompt: I'm seeing a brand_name of 'UNKNOWN' in the gold layer. Pull the live rows from stg_products where brand_name is null or 'UNKNOWN'.
examplePrompts.intent: Investigate a suspicious value with a live read-only query.

examplePrompts.prompt: Compare my dev build of dim_customers against production using deferred state at target-prod/ and tell me if anything broke before I open the PR.
examplePrompts.intent: Dev-vs-prod drift validation for safe PR merge using deferred state.

examplePrompts.prompt: Run dbt show on int_orders_enriched after my macro change and confirm the support_ticket_count column actually populates.
examplePrompts.intent: Verify a macro/ref change produces correct data, not just valid SQL.

examplePrompts.prompt: My fct_orders change should only add rows, not change existing totals. Diff dev vs prod on order_id and net_revenue and confirm.
examplePrompts.intent: Confirm an edit is additive (non-breaking) using key and column-scoped diff.

interaction.scenario: An SE has edited int_orders_enriched to enrich it with support ticket data and wants to preview the live output, sanity-check a suspicious null, and confirm the change is additive against prod before opening a PR.
interaction.userPrompt: I just added support_ticket_count to int_orders_enriched. Preview the real output, check whether any order_id is missing a count, and confirm this only adds a column without changing existing rows before I merge.

events.body: I just added support_ticket_count to int_orders_enriched. Preview the real output, check whether any order_id is missing a count, and confirm this only adds a column without changing existing rows before I merge.

I'll resolve the model's Jinja and run it on the warehouse to see real rows, then spot-check the new column for nulls, then diff dev against prod keyed on order_id. Starting with dbt_show so refs and macros are fully resolved.
dbt_show resolved both refs and executed on the warehouse. The new support_ticket_count column populates, but ORD-1002 came back null.
A null on support_ticket_count means the join to stg_support_tickets isn't producing 0 for orders with no tickets. Let me quantify how many order_ids are affected with a live read-only query.

events.toolArgs: {"sql":"select count(*) as null_count from analytics_dev.silver.int_orders_enriched where support_ticket_count is null","limit":1}

events.body: 3,174 orders have a null instead of 0. That's a left join that should coalesce to 0. This is a data-correctness issue, not a syntax one, so it would never surface from reading the file. Now I'll diff dev vs prod keyed on order_id to confirm the rest of the change is additive, using deferred state at target-prod/ to resolve the prod side.

events.code: {"rows_dev":48210,"rows_prod":48210,"added":0,"removed":0,"changed":0,"new_columns":["support_ticket_count"],"assessment":"non-breaking on existing columns; one added column"}

events.body: Validation subagent confirms: row count identical (48,210), zero changed/added/removed rows on order_id, customer_id, and order_total. The only structural change is the added support_ticket_count column.

Live results: the change is additive in shape, dev and prod both have 48,210 rows with zero changes to order_id, customer_id, or order_total, and the only new column is support_ticket_count. But there is a real data bug: 3,174 orders have support_ticket_count = null instead of 0, because the left join to stg_support_tickets isn't coalescing. Wrap the count in coalesce(..., 0) before you merge. Once fixed, re-run dbt_show to confirm ORD-1002 returns 0 and you're safe to open the PR.

seNotes: Lead the demo with the file-vs-warehouse contrast: a general coding agent says 'the SQL looks fine,' dbt Wizard says '3,174 rows are null in the actual warehouse.' That gap is the whole pitch.

dbt_show is not dbt compile: it resolves Jinja AND executes the compiled SQL on the warehouse, so it returns real rows and catches valid-but-wrong-data bugs that valid compiled SQL would still hide. Call that out explicitly.

Per dbt Wizard's own reference, diff requires either dbt Cloud or deferred state (--defer --state) pointed at a production manifest/artifacts path. Both are valid. In a local CLI demo, use the deferred-state path: stage a prod manifest at target-prod/ before you demo or the prod side won't resolve.

Stress that the warehouse tool is read-only by design: it issues SELECT-only statements and rejects DML/DDL at the tool layer, and pairing it with a least-privilege read role gives defense in depth. This is what lets you run live exploration against a customer-like warehouse with no write risk.

Emphasize deferred state for cost: diff and validation reuse prod upstreams instead of rebuilding the DAG, which matters on large Snowflake/BigQuery projects where a full rebuild is expensive. Deferred state is the cost-efficient path; dbt Cloud state is the alternative way to resolve prod.
The validation subagent runs the prod-vs-dev comparison automatically after an edit, so the safe-merge story isn't a manual step the SE has to remember.

tools.name: mcp__dbt_index__warehouse
tools.purpose: Execute read-only SQL directly against the connected warehouse for live data previews, row counts, and value spot-checks without running dbt.

params.name: sql
params.desc: SELECT-only statement to run against the warehouse. DML/DDL is rejected.

params.name: limit
params.desc: Optional cap on rows returned for fast previews (e.g. 100).

tools.returns: Result set as columns + rows with row count, plus the warehouse the query ran against. Read-only by design: SELECT-only, rejects DML/DDL.

tools.name: mcp__dbt_index__dbt_show
tools.purpose: Resolve a model's full Jinja ({{ ref() }}, {{ source() }}, macros) into executable SQL and run it on the warehouse to return the rows the model would emit, surfacing valid-but-wrong-data bugs that compiled-SQL inspection alone would miss.

params.name: node
params.desc: Model name or selector to compile and preview (e.g. int_orders_enriched).

params.name: limit
params.desc: Optional row limit for the preview.

params.name: state
params.desc: Optional path to deferred production state so unbuilt upstreams resolve to prod relations (e.g. target-prod/).

tools.returns: Compiled SQL plus the executed result rows from the warehouse, with the resolved ref/source relation names.

tools.name: mcp__dbt_index__diff
tools.signature: mcp__dbt_index__diff(node, state?, defer?, primary_key?, columns?, dimensions?)
tools.purpose: Compare dev state against production state for a node to surface row-count and value drift before merging a PR. Per dbt Wizard's own reference, diff requires either dbt Cloud or deferred state (--defer --state) pointed at a production manifest.

params.name: node
params.desc: Model to compare across dev and prod (e.g. fct_orders).

params.name: state
params.desc: Path to the production manifest/artifacts for deferred comparison (e.g. target-prod/). Used with defer when not running against dbt Cloud.

params.name: defer
params.desc: Reuse production upstreams instead of rebuilding the full DAG. Pairs with state for the deferred-state path; dbt Cloud is the alternative source of prod state.

params.name: primary_key
params.desc: Primary key column(s) used to align dev and prod rows.

params.name: columns
params.desc: Optional subset of columns to compare for value drift.

params.name: dimensions
params.desc: Optional dimensions to group the comparison by.

tools.returns: Row-count delta (added/removed/changed), per-column value differences, and a breaking/non-breaking assessment. Resolves the prod side via dbt Cloud OR deferred state (--defer --state) pointed at a production manifest; both are valid.
